title: Kitesurfing Rhodes — Prasonisi Spot Guide 2026
meta_title: Kitesurfing Rhodes Prasonisi 2026 | Spot Guide
meta_description: Discover kitesurfing Rhodes Prasonisi: consistent winds, shallow lagoon, summer thermal thermals. Best season, gear tips & local access. Find it at Surf...
excerpt: Rhodes Prasonisi offers consistent thermal winds, shallow warm water, and perfect conditions for kitesurfing across summer—ideal for progression and freestyle.
body_html:
Why Rhodes — Prasonisi, Greece Is Worth the Trip

Prasonisi is one of Europe's most reliable and accessible kitesurfing Rhodes Prasonisi destinations. Located at the southern tip of Rhodes, this dramatic peninsula is bordered by two bays—the Aegean Sea to the east and the Mediterranean to the west—creating a natural funnel that accelerates thermal wind almost every afternoon between May and September. The lagoon side (west) offers shallow, flat water perfect for learning and freestyle tricks, whilst the open side delivers stronger swells for wave riding. Water temperatures hover around 23–26°C in summer, meaning lighter wetsuits or spring suits keep you comfortable through long sessions. The beach is well-developed, with numerous tavernas, easy parking, and a thriving international kite community that gathers year-round.

Best Spots & Access

Prasonisi's primary launch point is on the lagoon (west/Mediterranean side), where shallow water and sand banks make entry safe and hassle-free. Park at the main beach car park (free or small fee), walk down to the water, and set up within metres of the shore. The lagoon stretches roughly 2 km across and 1 km out; wind lines are gentle, and the seabed rises gradually, making it ideal for kit control drills and progression.

For more challenge and swell, head to the eastern (Aegean) shore. Access is via the same car park; the open beach demands stronger conditions and experience with offshore wind patterns. Watch for shipping lanes in deep water and stay aware of local fishing activity. Summer thermals can gust suddenly; always have a safety team and use a reliable chicken loop and quick-release system.

Hazard note: Prasonisi can crowd quickly in peak season; arrive early (07:00–09:00) to secure space and enjoy cleaner wind before thermal convection peaks.

Wind & Weather by Season

Month Avg Wind (knots) Direction Water Temp (°C) Conditions Rating
January 12–16 NW 16 Inconsistent
February 14–18 NW 15 Moderate
March 14–20 NW 16 Good
April 13–18 N–NW 18 Good
May 16–22 N–NE (thermal) 21 Excellent
June 18–25 N–NE (thermal) 23 Excellent
July 20–28 N–NE (thermal) 25 Outstanding
August 19–26 N–NE (thermal) 26 Outstanding
September 16–23 N–NE 24 Excellent
October 12–18 NW 21 Good
November 10–16 NW 19 Moderate
December 11–15 NW 17 Inconsistent

Peak season (May–September) offers the most reliable winds. Thermal systems build from around 11:00, reach peak strength by 15:00–17:00, and fade by sunset. Morning sessions can be light; afternoons guarantee 18+ knots on the lagoon side.

What Gear to Bring

  • Kites: 14–17 m² for light thermals (May, early June); 12–15 m² for peak summer wind. A Duotone Evo or Cabrinha FX gives responsive control in variable conditions. Consider a quad-setup or hybrid for efficient use of the entire wind range.
  • Boards: A 135–150 L wave/freestyle hybrid (JP Australia Pro or Nobile NHP) suits Prasonisi's shallow lagoon and occasional swell. Smaller, stiffer boards (120–135 L) work better when chop builds in afternoon wind.
  • Wetsuits: Spring suit (2 mm) or rashguard June–August; 3/2 mm suit for May and September; 4/3 mm for winter (if visiting November–March). ION makes durable, flexible suits ideal for European spots.
  • Safety: Always use a helmet, a waist-mounted knife, and a reliable spreader bar. The lagoon is forgiving, but rips and sudden gusts demand respect.

Rent or Buy? Advice from Surf Store

First-time kitesurfing Rhodes Prasonisi visitors often rent locally—several schools and shops line the beach. Rental gear is serviceable but aging; if you already own a kit, bring it. For serious progression or multi-week stays, buying a used kite and board from Surf Store's stock of premium European secondhand gear makes sense financially and ensures you trust your equipment on the water. We stock Duotone, Cabrinha, NeilPryde, and Nobile gear trusted by Greek riders and dispatch across Europe. If you're committing to Prasonisi trips year-round, investing in a personal 14 m² and 12 m² kite (summer/spring rotation) plus a hybrid 140 L board pays dividends within 2–3 seasons.

title: Kitesurfing Paros — Golden Beach Spot Guide 2026
meta_title: Kitesurfing Paros Golden Beach Greece 2026 Guide

excerpt: Discover why Paros Golden Beach is Europe's premier kitesurfing destination with consistent winds, shallow waters, and world-class infrastructure.
body_html:
Why Paros — Golden Beach, Greece Is Worth the Trip

Kitesurfing Paros Golden Beach, Greece attracts riders from across Europe for one simple reason: it delivers reliable conditions almost year-round. The beach faces northeast, capturing thermal winds that build predictably through the afternoon. Water is warm enough to ride in just boardshorts or a thin wetsuit for half the year, and the shallow, sandy bay forgives mistakes—perfect for progressing your trick technique without fear.

Golden Beach itself is a 2 km crescent of golden sand backed by a developing scene of kite schools, cafés, and rental shops. The vibe is chilled but professional; you'll find serious riders pushing their limits alongside beginners learning their first jumps. The seabed is sand and fine shale, with no rocks or urchins to worry about. Summer crowds are real, but the bay is big enough that even in peak season you'll find room to work.

Beyond the water, Paros offers character. The island has authentic tavernas, good local wine, and whitewashed villages in the hills. Naoussa, the traditional fishing town 10 km north, is worth an evening. Transport is straightforward from Athens or other Greek islands, and accommodation ranges from beachside hotels to family-run rooms at fair prices.

Best Spots & Access

Golden Beach is the hub. The main launch area is in front of Paros Kitesurfing and other schools—look for the flags and windsock. Parking is free on the beach road; arrive early in summer to secure a spot near the water. The beach shelves gently for the first 200 m, ideal for learning and flat-water freestyle. Beyond that, small wind-driven chop builds, and the seabed transitions to sand bars that shift seasonally.

For stronger wind days or if you want more solitude, Logaras Beach (3 km south) offers similar conditions with fewer riders. It's slightly more exposed and sees better wind in light days. Both beaches have organised facilities: showers, cafés, and secure areas to leave gear.

Hazards are minimal but real. Occasional rocks sit in the shallows near the northern end of Golden Beach—local riders know to avoid that zone. Summer pleasure-boat traffic can be heavy; stay alert and respect kite-free zones marked by buoys. The thermal wind can die abruptly in late afternoon, stranding you on the water if you're not vigilant about timing your session.

Wind & Weather by Season

Month Avg Wind (knots) Direction Water Temp (°C) Ridability Rating
January 12–16 NW–N 14 Good
February 12–15 NW–N 13 Good
March 10–14 N–NE 14 Fair
April 11–15 N–NE 16 Good
May 13–17 NE 19 Excellent
June 14–18 NE 22 Excellent
July 15–19 NE 24 Excellent
August 14–18 NE 25 Excellent
September 12–16 NE–E 23 Very Good
October 11–15 NE–N 20 Good
November 11–14 NW–N 17 Fair–Good
December 12–15 NW–N 15 Good

The best season for kitesurfing Paros Golden Beach, Greece is May through September. The thermal Etesian wind blows reliably 14–19 knots, starting around midday and building through afternoon. Water is warm enough for minimal protection. Winter (December–February) still offers solid riding, but sessions are shorter and the water demands a 4/3 mm wetsuit. Spring and autumn are underrated; crowds thin, and wind remains consistent.

What Gear to Bring

Pack a quiver of kite sizes: 17 m for light-wind days (10–12 knots), a workhorse 14 m for the typical 14–17 knot days, and a 12 m for when the thermal really fires. The 14 m will cover 70 % of your sessions. A directional or light freestyle board (around 140 L) suits Golden Beach's flat water perfectly—you'll progress tricks faster than on a wave board.

For wetsuits: bring a shorty or rashguard for summer (June–August), and a 3/2 mm for spring and autumn. Winter riders need a 4/3 mm or 5/4 mm. The water cools to 13–14 °C in January and February.

Don't forget a good helmet, UV shirt, and a repair kit (spare bridle line, adhesive, patches). The sun is intense, even on cloudy days. Check out our selection of kitesurfing gear at Surf Store before you go—brands like Duotone and Cabrinha are stocked and ready to ship to Greece if you need to top up your quiver.

Rent or Buy? Advice from Surf Store

If you're a regular visitor or live nearby, buy. A quality setup—17 m, 14 m, and 12 m kites plus a board—costs €2,000–€3,500 new. You'll use it dozens of times a season and own gear that suits your style. Rental at Golden Beach costs €35–€50 per day, so ownership breaks even in 40–100 days of riding.

If you're visiting once a year, rent locally. The schools at Golden Beach stock modern equipment from reputable brands. That said, if you already own gear, flying with it or having it shipped via Surf Store beforehand ensures you get exactly what you're comfortable on.

Our tip: buy your core 14 m kite and a board from Surf Store before your trip, ship them to your accommodation, and rent the 17 m and 12 m locally. It's the best of both worlds. Visit Surf Store to browse our Duotone, Cabrinha, and NeilPryde range—our team can advise you on the perfect setup for Paros conditions.

title: Windsurfing Naxos — Mikri Vigla Spot Guide 2026

excerpt: Mikri Vigla on Naxos delivers reliable Etesian winds, shallow water entry, and a welcoming community—ideal for windsurfing and kitesurfing year-round.
body_html:
Why Naxos — Mikri Vigla, Greece Is Worth the Trip

Mikri Vigla sits on the east coast of Naxos, one of the Cyclades' largest islands, and offers something rare in the Mediterranean: consistent, predictable wind and forgiving water conditions. The bay faces north-east, capturing the famous Etesian winds that blow reliably from June through September and return sporadically throughout the year. What makes windsurfing and kitesurfing at Naxos Mikri Vigla special is the combination of accessible shallow water for learning, deeper off-shore conditions for advanced riders, and a tight-knit international community that gathers here each season.

The water is warm, the scenery is quintessentially Cycladic—whitewashed villages visible from the beach—and the local tavernas serve fresh seafood steps away from your kit. Unlike crowded tourist beaches, Mikri Vigla remains relatively quiet, with dedicated water sports enthusiasts who respect the spot and each other.

Best Spots & Access

Mikri Vigla beach is roughly 10 km south-east of Naxos Town. The main beach has a long, gently sloping sandy bottom, ideal for launching both kites and windsurfing boards. Park near the small taverna at the north end of the beach; the walk to the water is under 100 metres.

  • Launch Point: The shallow bay between the rocks at the north end offers protected conditions for learning. Deeper, cleaner wind kicks in 200–300 metres offshore.
  • Hazards: Rocky outcrops at the southern end of the beach are marked but require awareness. Strong wind can create choppy conditions beyond the bay; watch for sudden pressure increases mid-afternoon.
  • Access: A concrete access road serves the beach; no entry fees. Summer parking can be tight mid-afternoon, so arrive early.

Wind & Weather by Season

Month Avg Wind (knots) Direction Water Temp (°C) Spot Rating
January 12–18 NE 16 Fair
February 14–20 NE 15 Good
March 12–16 NE 16 Good
April 10–15 NE 18 Fair
May 12–18 NE 20 Good
June 16–25 NE 23 Excellent
July 18–28 NE 25 Excellent
August 16–26 NE 25 Excellent

November 12–18 NE–W 18 Fair
December 12–20 NE–W 16 Fair

The Etesian winds are strongest June through September, making summer the peak season. Winter is quieter but still rideable; the advantage is fewer crowds and calmer mornings before the wind arrives by midday.

What Gear to Bring

For kitesurfing at Naxos Mikri Vigla: Pack 14–17 m² kites for summer (June–September); drop to 12–14 m² in shoulder seasons. A twintip board (36–41 litres) or hydrofoil works best. Summer water needs only swim shorts; in winter, a 3/2 mm wetsuit keeps you comfortable.

For windsurfing: Bring a 75–90 litre all-round wave or freestyle board, paired with 4.5–6.0 m² sails depending on the season. High-summer conditions favour smaller, more responsive sails (4.5–5.2 m²); winter and shoulder seasons need 5.5–6.0 m². A 3/2 mm winter suit (November–March) and boardshorts for summer round out your kit.

Both disciplines benefit from a helm or cap—the sun is intense, and wind can catch you off-guard. Reef shoes help navigate the rocky exit point.

Rent or Buy? Advice from Surf Store

If you are visiting for a week or less, rental outfits on Naxos can cover basic gear. However, quality and stock vary; many riders end up frustrated by mismatched equipment or worn kites. We recommend buying from Surf Store before you travel. Our Duotone, Cabrinha, NeilPryde, and Fanatic boards and kites are chosen by riders who know the Aegean well. Shipping across Europe means your favourite kit arrives tuned and ready—no surprises on arrival. For windsurfing, our JP Australia and Tabou boards are renowned for light-wind performance and durability in salt water.

If you already own gear, check condition before you go: UV and salt degrade materials faster than you expect. Visit Surf Store to refresh your quiver or grab spares—a broken boom or torn batten can end your session.

title: Windsurfing Kos — Marmari Spot Guide 2026

body_html:
Why Kos—Marmari, Greece Is Worth the Trip

Marmari, on the southeastern coast of Kos, is one of Europe's most reliable windsurfing destinations. The long, protected bay creates a natural wind-funnelling corridor where thermal breezes build predictably—especially during summer months—making it ideal for both beginners learning to harness and advanced riders pushing their limits in stronger swells.

The combination of consistent winds, warm Mediterranean water, shallow launch zones, and a welcoming local community makes windsurfing Kos Marmari Greece a year-round playground. Unlike many Greek spots, Marmari offers shelter from the meltemia (summer northerly winds) that blast other islands, yet still captures enough pressure to deliver quality sessions. The bay's sandy bottom and gradual depth are forgiving for learning, while the open water beyond suits progression and freestyle.

Water temperature rarely dips below 15°C, even in winter, and summer sees 24–26°C seas. Combined with authentic Greek hospitality, excellent local food, and direct flights to Kos, Marmari punches well above its weight.

Best Spots & Access

The main launch beach at Marmari is a wide, sandy strip running along the bay's northern shore. Park near the taverna cluster or in the small lot behind the beach—both are free and rarely crowded. The beach itself is public; no permits required.

  • Main launch: Centre beach (coordinates available via Google Maps "Marmari Windsurfing Beach"). Shallow for 50–100 metres, ideal for getups and first runs.
  • Advanced zone: Eastern end of the bay, where the beach narrows and swells wrap in from the south—good for bump-and-jump in 15+ knots.
  • Hazards: Occasional fishing nets in the bay; check with locals before each session. A few scattered rocks near the eastern rocks—respect the warnings.
  • Facilities: Tavernas, showers, and cafés dot the beach. Mobile signal is good; there's a small supermarket 500 metres inland.

February 9–13 NW–N 15 ★★★☆☆

December 7–11 NW–N 16 ★★☆☆☆

Peak season: May–September. The meltemia brings steady NE winds 12–16 knots most afternoons; thermals develop by 10 a.m. and peak at 2–4 p.m. Winter is lighter and gustier but still rideable for intermediate sailors.

What Gear to Bring

For windsurfing Kos Marmari Greece, pack sails and boards scaled to seasonal conditions.

  • Summer (May–September): 4.5–5.5 m² sails for thermals and strong meltemia. Boards: 75–90 litres, wide and stable for freeride and wave play.
  • Winter (November–March): 5.5–7.0 m² sails for lighter northwesterlies. Boards: 100–120 litres, high volume for easy water starts.
  • Wetsuits: 3/2 mm (spring–autumn); 4/3 mm (autumn–spring). A shorty suffices in mid-summer; many go barefoot in July–August.
  • Accessories: Harness, gloves (winter), booties (rocky launch areas), and UV protection essential—the sun is strong year-round.

Rent or Buy? Advice from Surf Store

If you're visiting Marmari for a week or two, rent locally—several shops near the beach offer mid-range sails and boards at fair prices. However, if you're a regular or planning multiple trips, purchasing quality gear through Surf Store ensures you ride kit optimised for your weight, skill, and local conditions. We stock Duotone, Cabrinha, and Fanatic boards with European pricing and expert guidance; shipping across Europe is reliable and fast. A personal quiver always beats rental availability and lets you progress faster on a spot you know.

title: Kitesurfing Mykonos — Kalafatis Beach Guide 2026

excerpt: Kalafatis delivers consistent wind, flat water, and stunning Greek scenery—a premier Mediterranean kitesurfing destination.
body_html:
Why Mykonos — Kalafatis, Greece Is Worth the Trip

Kalafatis beach on Mykonos is one of Greece's most reliable kitesurfing destinations. Sheltered by surrounding hills and positioned to catch the famous Etesian winds that dominate the Aegean from June through August, Kalafatis offers consistent thermal and pressure-driven wind that keeps riders on the water when other Mediterranean spots sit flat. The bay itself provides forgiving conditions: relatively flat water, sandy bottom, and a shallow launch area perfect for both learning and progression.

Beyond the water, Kalafatis combines serious wind with the charm of a working Greek fishing village. You'll find tavernas serving fresh seafood, affordable accommodation, and a welcoming local community that understands water sports culture. The beach is long and sandy, with good facilities and space to rig without crowding. For kitesurfing Mykonos Kalafatis enthusiasts, it's an escape from the island's party-focused reputation into an authentic, wind-powered community.

Best Spots & Access

The main kitesurfing launch is at Kalafatis central beach, where the municipal area has an informal kite school and rental station. Parking is straightforward: a large gravel car park sits directly behind the beach, costing €3–5 per day. From the car park, it's a 50-metre walk to the sand.

For intermediate and advanced riders, the southern end of the bay—near the fish farms—offers slightly stronger wind acceleration when the Etesian blows hard. Access is via a rougher track; a 4WD or high-clearance car helps. The water remains shallow and forgiving across the entire bay (1–3 metres), so spot selection depends more on wind strength and crowd preference than hazard avoidance.

Watch for fishing nets and small boats in the bay, especially in the early morning. The prevailing wind is northerly; launching is safest from the central beach where local knowledge is strongest. Avoid launching directly in front of the fish farm structures at the bay's southern point. In July and August, the beach can attract casual swimmers; a courteous approach helps maintain the spot's reputation.

The Etesian winds peak June–August, delivering reliable 15–20 knot pressure with thermal reinforcement in the afternoon. Spring (May–June) and autumn (September–October) offer solid conditions with fewer tourists. Winter is rideable but inconsistent; pack a thicker wetsuit.

What Gear to Bring

For kitesurfing at Mykonos Kalafatis, pack kites in the 14–17 m² range for summer and 12–15 m² for shoulder seasons. Light-wind days (under 10 knots) benefit from a 17 m² if you're progressing; strong Etesian afternoons (18+ knots) suit 12–14 m². A directional or freeride board (140–150 litres) handles the bay's flat water and gives confidence in variable wind.

Wetsuits: bring a 3/2 mm spring suit for summer and a 5/3 mm winter suit for November–March. Water temperature climbs to 26°C in August but drops to 12°C by February. Booties help on rocky launch areas; a helmet is advisable given the shallow bay's busy character.

Rent or Buy? Advice from Surf Store

Mykonos has local rental shops, but quality and maintenance vary. If you're visiting for a week or longer, buying fresh gear from Surf Store before you arrive—or renting high-end equipment for the duration—pays dividends. body_html:
Why Lefkada — Vassiliki, Greece Is Worth the Trip

Vassiliki Bay, on the southern tip of Lefkada island, is one of Europe's most consistent and beginner-friendly windsurfing destinations. The bay's geography creates a natural funnel for thermal winds that build reliably every afternoon between April and October, making windsurfing Lefkada Vassiliki Greece a predictable choice for planning your trip. Unlike Atlantic spots dependent on swell patterns, Vassiliki rewards patience: you can often sail from midday onwards with increasing intensity.

The shallow, sheltered bay keeps water temperatures mild and provides forgiving conditions for learning progression. The beach community is relaxed but professional—local schools know the spot intimately, and the bay's orientation means you're never far from help. Summer thermals can reach 25 knots by mid-afternoon, while winter offers lighter 12–18 knot winds ideal for larger boards and sails.

Best Spots & Access

Vassiliki's main launch beach runs along the entire bay, with dedicated windsurfing schools marking their zones clearly. Park near the village (free spaces in summer are rare; arrive early or use the paid lot 200 m inland). The beach shelves gradually for 40 m, making rigging and launching stress-free even with full gear.

  • Main Launch Beach: Eastern shoreline, 500 m of sand, best for all levels. Wind funnels from the northwest; thermals build from noon.
  • Geni Island: Small islet 1 km offshore; experienced sailors drift here in strong afternoons. Not recommended for beginners.
  • Hazards: Fishing nets occasionally anchored in the bay; check with locals daily. Afternoon thermals can turn gusty by 16:00—plan your session accordingly.

What Gear to Bring

Thermal winds at Vassiliki favour waves sails and freestyle boards, but windsurfing Lefkada Vassiliki Greece accommodates all disciplines. Pack a quiver to match the season:

  • Sails: May–September: 4.0–5.5 m² for consistent thermals. Winter: 5.8–7.0 m² for lighter conditions. Duotone and NeilPryde models dominate the bay schools.
  • Boards: Wave or freestyle (90–100 L) for summer; freeride or cruiser (110–130 L) for winter and learning.
  • Wetsuits: 3/2 mm March–May and September–October; 2/1 mm June–August; 5/3 mm December–February.

Most schools rent complete rigs (sail + mast + boom + board), but bringing your own gear ensures familiarity. Ion and Gaastra wetsuits are available through Surf Store if you arrive without one.

Shipping to Greece takes 5–7 days; order before your trip. title: Kitesurfing Limnos — Keros Spot Guide 2026
meta_title: Kitesurfing Limnos Keros Beach Greece 2026 | Surf Store

excerpt: Keros Beach on Limnos offers reliable Aegean wind, shallow turquoise waters, and perfect conditions for intermediate to advanced kitesurfing year-round.
body_html:
Why Limnos — Keros Beach, Greece Is Worth the Trip

Keros Beach is one of the Aegean's best-kept kitesurfing secrets. Located on the northern coast of Limnos, this sprawling sandy beach combines consistent thermal and pressure-driven winds with shallow, warm waters and virtually no crowd pressure. Unlike crowded Mediterranean spots, you'll find space to push your skills—whether you're working on your first tricks or chasing your personal best.

The shallow bay offers forgiving conditions for progression. Water clarity rivals anywhere in Greece, and the beach's natural orientation funnels wind perfectly. Limnos itself is quiet and authentic; you'll stay in a traditional fishing village with excellent local tavernas and affordable accommodation. Kitesurfing Limnos at Keros Beach means real freedom on the water.

Best Spots & Access

Keros Beach stretches nearly 3 km along the northern shore. The main launch zone is the central beach area, where shallow water extends 100+ metres offshore—ideal for land starts and progression. Park near the small taverna cluster at the beach entrance (free parking, sometimes busy in peak season). A 30-metre walk gets you to sand.

The beach shelves gently with fine sand and occasional small rocks near the waterline; a helmet and board shorts suffice on windy days. Check for local fishing nets in the bay during early morning; the eastern third of the beach tends to be cleaner. No formal lifeguard service, so self-rescue and buddy systems are essential. Mobile signal is good; the nearest town (Myrina) is 45 minutes by car if you need supplies or medical care.

The Etesian winds—a signature Aegean summer pattern—make June to August peak season for kitesurfing Limnos. Expect reliable 16–22 knot thermals, warm water, and full sunshine. May and September offer nearly as much wind with fewer tourists. Winter brings occasional larger swells and colder water (11–13 °C); serious riders can find excellent conditions, but sessions demand warmer wetsuits.

What Gear to Bring

For summer kitesurfing at Keros Beach, pack a 14–17 m² wave or freestyle kite (depending on your weight and preferred style); the consistent 16–20 knot summer wind favours mid-sized kites. Bring a dedicated wave or twin-tip board—the shallow water and sandy bottom reward manoeuvrability. A rash vest or thin 2/2 mm shorty is enough June–August; autumn and spring demand 3/2 mm suits. Winter requires a 5/3 mm chest-zip or hooded suit.

Reef booties protect against occasional rocks and sea urchins. A helmet is recommended, especially for tricks and when learning. If you're light (under 70 kg), consider a 16 m² kite for July–August; heavier riders may prefer 17 m².

title: Kitesurfing Crete — Elafonissi & Falasarna Guide 2026

meta_description: Discover Crete's west coast kitesurfing spots: Elafonissi, Falasarna & more. Wind, gear, and local tips. Shop at Surf Store.
excerpt: Crete's west coast delivers consistent wind, warm water, and stunning scenery for kitesurfing Elafonissi, Falasarna, and beyond.
body_html:
Why Crete — West Coast Spots, Greece Is Worth the Trip

Crete's west coast is a gem for water sports enthusiasts. The combination of consistent thermal and Etesian winds, turquoise shallow lagoons, and minimal crowds makes this region one of Greece's best-kept kitesurfing secrets. Unlike crowded Mediterranean hotspots, you'll find long sandy beaches, crystal-clear water, and room to progress at your own pace. The landscape is dramatic—white sand dunes, emerald bays, and mountains rising behind—giving every session a holiday feeling.

The shallow, protected waters at Elafonissi and Falasarna are ideal for beginners and freestyle enthusiasts. Intermediate and advanced riders benefit from stronger offshore winds further west. All year round, Crete offers reliable wind, warm water in summer, and manageable swell. Best of all, local hospitality and affordable accommodation make extended trips worthwhile.

Best Spots & Access

Elafonissi Beach is the headline spot on Crete's west coast. This protected lagoon sits in the far southwest, surrounded by pink sand and shallow water (0.5–2 metres). It's ideal for beginners and freestyle; the sand bottom is forgiving, and wind wraps around the bay. Park at the main beach car park (small fee); the lagoon launch is a 5-minute walk. Watch for rocks on the eastern edge and shallow sandbars when wind backs north.

Falasarna Beach, 40 km north, offers longer fetches and stronger wind. This large sandy bay faces northwest and catches pure Etesian flow. The beach is uncrowded with good parking. Launch from the southern end to avoid fishing nets. Swell can build in autumn; be mindful of undertow in heavy swells.

Livadakia & Kamari, nearby, provide alternative options during calm spells or when wind is too strong at Elafonissi. Both have gentle conditions and good amenities.

Access throughout is straightforward: main roads link Chania airport to the west coast (90–120 minutes by car). Local car hire is affordable. Bring an international driving licence and respect speed limits and parking regulations.

Kite Sizes: For Elafonissi, pack a 14–17 m² kite. In summer peak wind, a 12 m² handles gusts. For Falasarna and stronger spots, bring 12–15 m². Lighter riders benefit from larger kites in spring.

Board: A freestyle or wave board (135–145 L) suits shallow Elafonissi. For stronger conditions, a smaller wave board (120–135 L) works well.

Wetsuit: June–September: 3 mm spring suit or shorty. April–May and October: 4/3 mm. November–March: 5/3 mm or 6/4 mm hooded suit. Water stays warm, but sun exposure demands UV protection.

Located on Gran Canaria's east coast, this legendary spot delivers the most consistent wind conditions in Europe, backed by a thriving community of professionals, schools, and accommodation tailored to water sports enthusiasts. The combination of reliable north-east trade winds, protected bays with glassy flat water, and a sunny climate makes windsurfing Gran Canaria Pozo Izquierdo the go-to destination whether you're progressing your freestyle, boosting your wave skills, or simply chasing guaranteed wind.

The spot has hosted World Cups, championships, and countless film productions—not by accident, but because conditions here are simply unbeatable. You'll share the water with international coaches, sponsored athletes, and dedicated riders from across Europe, creating an environment where progression accelerates.

Best Spots & Access

Pozo Izquierdo itself is the main launch, a sandy beach backed by a car park just metres away. Entry is straightforward, shallow, and forgiving for all skill levels. The bay offers multiple conditions:

  • Flat-water freestyle zone — the western section near the town, ideal for tricks and light-wind sessions
  • Wave field — offshore swells wrap around the eastern end, perfect for wave riding in 15–25 knots
  • Powered-up sessions — when trade winds peak, the open bay handles 20+ knots with minimal chop

Parking is abundant and cheap (€2–3 per day). The beach is flanked by restaurants, shops, and dedicated windsurfing schools. Local hazards are minimal—the spot is well-patrolled, deep water drops off gradually, and reef or rock dangers are clearly marked. Currents can run, so always check conditions with the local school before launching.

title: Kitesurfing Lanzarote — La Santa Spot Guide 2026

body_html:
Why Lanzarote — La Santa, Spain Is Worth the Trip

La Santa on Lanzarote's north coast has become Europe's go-to kitesurfing destination, and for good reason. The bay offers consistent trade winds nearly year-round, flat-water conditions ideal for progression, and a tight-knit community of riders from across the continent. Unlike Atlantic-facing breaks that get crowded swell, kitesurfing Lanzarote La Santa depends on wind, and the geography delivers. The trade winds funnel down from the northeast with remarkable reliability, creating a natural wind corridor that rarely disappoints.

The village itself is understated—a handful of restaurants, a school, parking, and direct beach access. There's no pretence, just serious riders and perfect conditions. Water temperatures stay mild even in winter (around 17–18°C), and the infrastructure caters to kiters. If you're chasing guaranteed wind and want to log serious sessions without the unpredictability of Atlantic swells, kitesurfing Lanzarote La Santa is unbeatable.

Best Spots & Access

The main launch is at La Santa village beach itself—a wide, sandy bay with easy entry and exit. Parking is straightforward: there's a small car park right beside the beach, and overflow parking along the coastal road. The beach faces northeast, directly into the prevailing trade winds, so launch conditions are typically optimal.

Wind funnels from two directions here: the primary northeast trade wind and occasional Atlantic swells bring north-northwest gusts. The bay itself is protected from swell, making it perfect for foiling and freestyle. A secondary spot, Famara beach (about 8 km south), offers stronger, gustier wind and slightly more power if you want to push harder or practise wave riding on the occasional winter swell.

Hazards are minimal: watch for the occasional fishing line or buoy, and respect the swimmers and windsurfers who share the water. The seabed is sandy with occasional rocks near the southern headland—stay in the main bay for safety. Local kiters are friendly and will point out any daily quirks.

What Gear to Bring

Pack a quiver covering 12–17 knots of wind. Most riders bring a 15 m² and 12 m² kite; add a 17 m² if you're lighter or expect light days. Conditions rarely drop below 10 knots or exceed 25 knots, so you won't need extremes.

For boards, a directional wave board (around 55–65 litres) or a freestyle board (40–50 litres) works well. Foiling is popular here: bring a foil board and a light wind kite for those 10–12 knot days. Water temperature drops to 16°C in February, so pack a 3/2 mm or 4/3 mm winter wetsuit. In summer, a 2/2 mm suffices.

title: Kitesurfing Tenerife — El Médano Spot Guide 2026

body_html:
Why Tenerife — El Médano, Spain Is Worth the Trip

El Médano sits on Tenerife's south coast, blessed with one of Europe's most consistent wind patterns. Thanks to the north-east trade winds funnelled between Tenerife and Gran Canaria, kitesurfing windsurfing Tenerife El Médano delivers reliable conditions almost year-round. The shallow lagoon stretches for nearly 3 km, making it perfect for beginners learning to control their kites, whilst advanced riders enjoy flat water for freestyle and a deeper channel for wave riding when Atlantic swells wrap in.

The beach village itself is laid-back but vibrant—excellent seafood restaurants, a welcoming local community of water sports enthusiasts, and direct access to the beach mean you can be rigged and on the water within minutes of arriving. The water is warm by northern European standards, and the sand bar offers natural protection from chop on lighter wind days.

Kitesurfing windsurfing Tenerife El Médano also benefits from being part of a larger water sports ecosystem. Wing foiling is gaining ground here, and the infrastructure—multiple schools, repair shops, and rental outlets—rivals any European destination. Best of all, the spot remains more affordable than many Mediterranean hotspots and attracts serious athletes rather than casual crowds.

Best Spots & Access

El Médano's main launch is the central beach, adjacent to the promenade and seafood restaurants. Parking is straightforward—several public car parks within 50 metres of the shoreline. Entry fees are minimal or free, depending on the season.

For kitesurfing windsurfing, the lagoon itself is your primary zone. The western end near Pozo Rojo offers shallower water and lighter wind on marginal days; the eastern section (towards Montana Colorada) funnels more consistent breeze and suits intermediate to advanced riders. A natural sandbank creates a sheltered launch zone that's ideal for setting up and checking equipment.

Key access points:

  • Central Beach: main launch, best for beginners and schools.
  • Pozo Rojo (West): gentler, shallow water, light-wind refuge.
  • Montana Colorada (East): stronger wind channel, better for wave riding when Atlantic swells arrive.

Hazards are minimal: occasional fishing boats in deeper water, and the occasional curiosity from locals and tourists. A small rocky outcrop at the western end is easily avoided. Water entry is almost always safe; undertow is rare due to the lagoon's design.

Wind & Weather by Season

February 14–18 NE 19 Strong, reliable, some Atlantic swell
March 15–20 NE 20 Peak spring wind, excellent

September 12–16 NE 24 Trade winds return, autumn swell
October 14–18 NE 23 Strong and reliable
November 15–20 NE 22 Very consistent, best season
December 13–17 NE 20 Steady, occasional gusty days

What Gear to Bring

For kitesurfing, pack a quiver covering 12 to 17 knots. A 14m kite is your mainstay; add a 11m for stronger days and a 17m for summer lulls. Boards should range from a lightweight wave board (44–52 litres) to a stable freeride platform (60–70 litres) for learners.

Windsurfers should bring a 4.5–5.0m sail for spring and autumn, and a 4.0m for winter gusts. A 75–85 litre all-round board handles the lagoon's flat water perfectly.

Wetsuits: January to April and October to December call for a 3/2mm spring suit; June to August, a shorty or rash vest suffices. Water temperatures range from 19 °C in winter to 24 °C in summer. Booties are optional but recommended for rocky entry points.

Don't forget a repair kit, spare battens, and sunscreen—the Canarian sun is relentless.

Rent or Buy? Advice from Surf Store

If you're visiting for a week or less, local rental schools offer excellent equipment at €30–50 per day. Budget-conscious riders often buy second-hand gear locally or online, then sell before leaving. This keeps costs down and ensures you have a proven setup.

Why Mallorca, Spain Is Worth the Trip

Mallorca has earned its reputation as one of Europe's premier kitesurfing destinations. The island's position in the western Mediterranean creates reliable thermal and pressure-system winds that funnel across bays and coastal areas. Unlike many European spots, kitesurfing mallorca best spots offer warm water, consistent conditions, and a genuine year-round season. You'll find everything from sheltered bays perfect for learning to exposed reef breaks for advanced riders. Best Spots & Access

Pollença Bay (Port de Pollença): The flagship spot for kitesurfing mallorca best spots. A large, protected bay with consistent thermals, shallow launch areas, and relatively flat water in most conditions. Parking is straightforward near the town, and the beach is lined with shops and restaurants. Best for all levels; perfect in summer when thermals peak.

Son Serra de Marina: A wilder, more exposed beach on the north coast. Stronger wind and choppier conditions suit intermediate to advanced riders. Parking can be tight on weekends. Watch for rocks and a short swimming zone; stay aware of local regulations.

Alcúdia Bay: Large, shallow bay with steady wind funnelling from the northeast. Excellent for freestyle and wave riding when the swell wraps in. Plenty of beach access and nearby facilities in Alcúdia town. A favourite for those seeking longer sessions without the crowds of Pollença.

Cala Millor: East coast spot with thermal winds and beautiful turquoise water. More sheltered than northern beaches; ideal for spring and early summer. Busy in July and August, but worth it for the reliable conditions and soft sand launch.

body_html:
Why Formentera, Spain Is Worth the Trip

Formentera, the southernmost island of the Balearic archipelago, offers something increasingly rare in Mediterranean kitesurfing: shallow, protected lagoons with consistent thermal winds and warm, crystal-clear water. Unlike crowded mainland spots, Formentera remains relatively undiscovered by the global kite crowd, meaning uncrowded sessions even in peak summer.

The island's unique geography creates natural wind corridors between Ibiza and Africa. Thermal flows build reliably from late morning through afternoon, while the lagoons themselves—particularly around Estany Pudent and the Illetes area—provide ideal learning conditions with white sand floors visible at depth. You're also just a 30-minute ferry from Ibiza Town, making it easy to combine a kitesurfing holiday with the island's other attractions.

For serious riders, Formentera's kitesurfing lagoon and Ibiza conditions offer genuine progression in a setting where you won't battle 200 other kites. The water stays bathwater-warm from June through September, and even winter sessions require only a light 3/2mm wetsuit.

Best Spots & Access

Estany Pudent (West Lagoon): The primary kitesurfing destination. Shallow (1–3 metres), protected from Atlantic swell, with consistent afternoon thermals. Park near the village of Sant Ferran; walk or short drive to the water. Launch directly from the sand beach. Hazard: shallow reef patches—stick to marked channels and scout first.

Illetes Beach (North): Exposed to Tramontana (north) winds; excellent for winter sessions and advanced riders seeking more power. Parking available; easy beach launch. Can get choppy; check conditions before committing.

Cala Saona (South): Scenic but inconsistent winds due to island shelter. Better for swimming than kitesurfing in summer; worth checking only in spring or autumn when Atlantic swells push consistent breezes.

Access: Rent a car in Ibiza Town (essential—no reliable public transport to launch points). All spots are 20–40 minutes from the ferry terminal. Bring your passport for the inter-island crossing.

Kite sizes: 12–15m for spring/autumn; 15–17m for summer thermals; 9–12m for winter power sessions. Estany Pudent's protection means you can ride slightly larger kites than open-water conditions would suggest.

Board: A twin-tip or freeride board (135–145 litres) suits all conditions. Shallow lagoon work suits smaller, more responsive shapes; wider boards help in light thermal winds.

Wetsuits: Bring a 4/3mm for winter and spring, a 3/2mm for autumn, and a 2/2mm rash guard for June–August. Water temperature stays mild even in winter by northern European standards.

title: Kitesurfing Mar Menor — Los Alcázares Guide 2026

body_html:
Why Mar Menor — Los Alcázares, Spain Is Worth the Trip

Mar Menor is Europe's largest saltwater lagoon, and Los Alcázares sits right in the heart of the action. If you're seeking kitesurfing Mar Menor Los Alcázares Spain with reliable conditions, this is it. The lagoon's shallow depth—averaging 3–4 metres—means warm water year-round and a forgiving platform for learning. The protected bay setup shields you from Atlantic swell, so you're riding pure wind power rather than fighting chop. Local thermals drive consistent afternoon breezes, especially spring through autumn, making this a thermometer-friendly destination even in cooler months.

What sets this spot apart is the community. Los Alcázares has a genuine kitesurfing culture with schools, repair shops, and a thriving crew of locals and visitors. The town itself is compact and relaxed—accommodation is affordable, restaurants serve fresh seafood, and you can be on the water within 10 minutes of parking.

Best Spots & Access

Los Alcázares has several launch zones around the town's shoreline. The main beach at Playa Centro is your best bet for kitesurfing—it's wide, flat, and has dedicated water sports areas. Parking is straightforward; you'll find free or cheap paid spots within 100 metres of the launch. The beach shelves gradually, so even in light wind you can walk out 50+ metres in waist-deep water.

Secondary breaks include Playa del Paseo (west of town) and the eastern shoreline towards San Javier, though these can get busier in summer. Check local traffic: the lagoon is shared with tourists, swimmers, and fishing boats, so launch early (before 09:00) or in the afternoon thermal window (14:00–19:00) to avoid crowds.

Hazards are minimal here. The shallow water and soft sand bottom protect you from hard impacts, but watch for occasional seagrass and be respectful of swimming zones. Winter storms occasionally churn the water, reducing visibility, but conditions rarely become dangerous—more often just choppy.

What Gear to Bring

Pack kite sizes between 12–17 m². In spring and autumn, a 14 m² is your workhorse; in summer, you'll need 16–17 m² for marginal sessions. Winter blows hard, so 12–14 m² sees you through. A directional or freeride board (90–110 L) suits the flat water perfectly—this is not a wave spot, so volume over rocker is your friend.

Wetsuits: March–May and September–November need 3/2 mm thickness; June–August, a shorty or spring suit keeps you comfortable. January–February demands 4/3 mm or a winter rig. The water's gentle on thermals, but consistency matters when you're out for hours.

title: Kitesurfing Costa Brava — Best Spots Guide 2026
meta_title: Kitesurfing Costa Brava Spain 2026 | Guide & Tips
meta_description: Explore Costa Brava's best kitesurfing costa brava spain spots, wind conditions & seasons. Expert tips from Surf Store. Find gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Discover the best kitesurfing costa brava spain spots, seasonal winds, and essential gear advice from European water sports experts.
body_html:
Why Costa Brava, Spain Is Worth the Trip

Costa Brava delivers consistent Mediterranean wind, warm water, and a laid-back beach culture that makes every session memorable. The rocky coves and sandy stretches give you variety: flat-water practice areas for learning, and choppy reef breaks when you want more excitement. Unlike crowded Atlantic spots, Costa Brava offers elbow room and genuine Spanish hospitality. You'll find excellent seafood, affordable accommodation, and a genuine water sports community rather than pure tourism.

The region's geography—steep cliffs, natural breakwaters, and protected bays—creates microclimates that work for every level. Whether you're a beginner looking for shelter or an advanced rider chasing wave riding, kitesurfing costa brava spain delivers. Water clarity is superb, and the summer season runs reliably from May through September.

Best Spots & Access

Platja de Torroella is the most accessible and beginner-friendly launch. Shallow water, sandy bottom, steady breeze, and plenty of flat space make it ideal for progression. Free parking 100 metres away; facilities on the beach. Avoid the rocky headland to the south.

Platja de Favàritx (Menorca day trip, 90 minutes) offers stronger, more stable wind and a wilder vibe. Launch from the southern end; respect local restrictions near the nudist beach. Hazards include a rocky plateau—stay inshore until you're confident reading the breaks.

Empuriabrava Lagoon is your flat-water classroom. Sheltered, warm, and ideal for technique work. No waves, perfect for freestyle or race training. Free access; park near the promenade.

Cala Montgó offers moderate waves and good wind funnelling. Launch from the northern cove; watch for swimmers and fishing lines in summer.

Peak season: September–October. Avoid: July–early August (light, inconsistent). Best value: May and late August when locals return and tourist crowds thin.

What Gear to Bring

For kitesurfing costa brava spain conditions, bring two kites: 14m and 17m. The region's light-to-moderate wind means a 17m handles lulls; 14m gives you control in the steady autumn swells. Twin-tip freestyle boards (137–139cm) suit flat water; for wave chasing, add a dedicated wave board (120–135cm).

Wetsuits are essential despite Mediterranean warmth. A 3/2mm suit covers May–October comfortably; add a 5/3mm hooded for winter visits. The morning ocean is cooler than midday, and when you're wet and windy, chill sets in fast.

Pack booties (reef is sharp), a helmet, and a leash. Duotone, Cabrinha, and NeilPryde kites dominate here—reliable, trusted kit that handles the sea state. title: Kitesurfing Lagos Algarve — Spot Guide 2026

excerpt: Lagos offers reliable Atlantic winds, stunning golden cliffs, and excellent kitesurfing conditions year-round for all skill levels.
body_html:
Why Lagos — Algarve, Portugal Is Worth the Trip

Lagos is one of the Algarve's finest water sports destinations, and for kitesurfing it's genuinely special. The town sits on Portugal's southwestern coast where Atlantic swells meet consistent trade winds, creating ideal conditions for riders of all levels. Unlike some crowded European spots, Lagos combines excellent wind window with a relaxed atmosphere and stunning golden-cliff scenery that makes every session memorable.

The beaches around Lagos are protected from the worst Atlantic chop by local geography, yet they remain windy and well-organised for kitesurfing. You'll find a genuine community of local and travelling water sports enthusiasts here, plus reliable conditions that make it worth planning your holiday around. The town itself has excellent restaurants, accommodation, and equipment rental shops, so you can focus entirely on your sessions.

Kitesurfing Lagos Algarve Portugal has become increasingly popular because the spot delivers consistency without the pretension of more famous venues. Water sports shops here understand what you need, and the local vibe is welcoming to beginners and advanced riders alike.

Best Spots & Access

The main launch points for kitesurfing Lagos Algarve are concentrated on three key beaches:

  • Meia Praia — the longest beach in the area (4.5 km) with excellent flat-water zones near the river mouth. Ideal for beginners and freestyle riders. Parking available near the western end; launch from the sand bar when winds favour offshore/cross-offshore conditions.
  • Dona Ana Beach — smaller, more scenic spot with direct access from town. Best for intermediate riders; stronger wind here than Meia Praia. Parking is limited; arrive early in peak season.
  • Ponta da Piedade — dramatic cliffs frame this southern-facing beach. More exposed to Atlantic swell; popular with wave riders. Current can be strong; local knowledge recommended for first visits.

All spots share a common hazard: variable tidal range (up to 4 metres). Check tide times before launching; low tide exposes rocks and sandbars. Summer crowds (July–August) mean you may need to travel 10 km west to quieter alternatives like Praia do Camilo if you prefer space.

Parking is straightforward at Meia Praia and Dona Ana; use the main seafront car parks. No launch fees, though beach clubs may ask for a small contribution if you use their facilities.

Wind & Weather by Season

Lagos benefits from Atlantic trade winds that run northeasterly most of the year, making it one of the most reliable spots for kitesurfing Lagos Algarve Portugal. Here's what to expect:

Peak season is March–May and October–November when wind is strongest and most consistent. Summers (July–August) are lighter but still rideable; water is warmest then. Winter brings occasional Atlantic storms but also reliable northeasterly swells.

What Gear to Bring

For kitesurfing Lagos Algarve Portugal, pack a quiver rather than relying on one setup:

  • Kite sizes: 14m and 17m for average winds (12–16 knots). Add a 12m for strong March–May sessions and a 19m for summer light-wind days. A reusable fabric kite (Duotone, Cabrinha, NeilPryde) suits the salt air better than cheaply made alternatives.
  • Boards: A 135–150 litre directional board works year-round for wave and flat-water riding. If freestyle appeals, consider a smaller 130 litre freestyle/freeride hybrid.
  • Wetsuits: A 3/2 mm spring suit (April–September) and 4/3 mm winter suit (November–March). November–February water temps (14–16°C) demand real warmth; ION makes excellent neoprene suited to Atlantic chill.
  • Safety: Helmet, impact vest, and a leash rated for your kite size. Lagos's rocks and cliffs mean safety gear isn't optional.

If you're visiting Lagos for a week or two, renting is sensible: local shops stock quality Duotone and Cabrinha kites with recent certification and proper maintenance. Rental costs €30–50 daily for kite, board, and harness.

body_html:
Why Peniche, Portugal Is Worth the Trip

Peniche sits on Portugal's rugged western coast, exposed directly to Atlantic swells and prevailing northerly winds that make it one of Europe's most consistent kitesurfing destinations. What sets kitesurfing in Peniche, Portugal apart is the blend of reliability, variety, and accessibility. You'll find everything from sheltered lagoon-style conditions for beginners to powerful reef breaks and beach launches that challenge intermediate and advanced riders.

The town itself is a working fishing port with a genuine community feel—not overly touristy, but with enough infrastructure to support water sports enthusiasts. Local schools, friendly locals, and a cluster of restaurants and cafés along the waterfront make it easy to spend weeks here. Most importantly, Peniche offers multiple launch points within 20 minutes of each other, so you can adapt to daily wind and swell conditions without long drives.

The water quality is generally excellent, visibility is good for reef spotting, and the surrounding cliffs provide stunning views. If you're serious about kitesurfing in Peniche, Portugal, you're choosing one of the Atlantic's most reliable training and progression grounds.

Best Spots & Access

Baleal Beach is the flagship launch point. A wide, sandy beach with natural breakwaters and a lagoon to the north creates a protected zone perfect for beginners and light-wind days. Parking is abundant, there's a beach bar, and the sandy bottom means softer landings. Access is straightforward: drive into the small village and follow signs to the praia (beach).

Supertubos Beach offers more power and is favoured by intermediate to advanced riders. The beach faces north-northwest and catches heavier swells. It's exposed, so only launch when conditions are solid and you're confident with stronger wind. Park near the cliffside restaurants and walk down—about five minutes.

Lagoon de Óbidos (30 minutes south) is the ideal flat-water playground. When Atlantic wind howls, the lagoon remains relatively sheltered, making it perfect for trick practice and light-wind sessions. Access is via the small town of Óbidos; local schools operate here and can point you to launch zones.

Safety notes: Peniche's reefs are sharp—wear reef boots. Watch for local fishing activity, especially at dawn. Check tide times before launching; some spots have strong currents. The Atlantic can surprise you with sudden swell increases, so respect the locals' advice and start conservatively if you're unfamiliar with the spot.

Wind & Weather by Season

Peniche's wind pattern is dominated by Atlantic systems and the Portuguese trade winds. Here's what to expect month by month:

Winter is the most reliable for steady northerlies but requires a 4/3 mm wetsuit. Summer is lighter and warmer but less dependable for wind.

What Gear to Bring

Kite sizes: Pack a 14 m² and a 17 m² for year-round conditions. In winter (14–18 knots), the 17 m² handles marginal days; in summer light winds, a 14 m² or smaller keeps you powered. For stormy Atlantic days (20+ knots), drop to 12 m² or 11 m².

Boards: A freestyle/all-round board (135–145 L for riders 70–85 kg) works well at Baleal and the lagoon. If you're tackling Supertubos and reef waves, bring a wave board (95–115 L) for control in chop and better hold in turns.

Wetsuits: December–March: 5/4 mm chest-zip (water 12–14°C). April–May and September–November: 4/3 mm. June–August: 3/2 mm shorty or rashguard. Peniche's Atlantic water stays cool year-round, so don't underestimate thickness.

Accessories: Reef boots are non-negotiable. A board leash, chicken loop backup, and a quality harness make a difference in consistent wind. body_html:
Why Viana do Castelo, Portugal Is Worth the Trip

Viana do Castelo sits on Portugal's northern coast where the Atlantic Ocean meets the Minho estuary, creating a unique blend of beach breaks, river launches, and protected bays. Unlike the crowded tourist spots further south, kitesurfing Viana do Castelo Portugal offers genuine, uncrowded sessions with consistent trade winds and friendly local riders who still remember when the sport was underground here.

The town itself is beautiful—a medieval riverside port with excellent restaurants, affordable accommodation, and a genuine culture of water sports. You're not just coming for the wind; you're joining a tight-knit community that takes the sport seriously. The mix of beach conditions, river-mouth launches, and bay-side flat-water spots means there's something for every skill level on any given day.

Best Spots & Access

Praia do Cabedelo (main beach launch) is the flagship spot for kitesurfing Viana do Castelo Portugal. It's a north-facing 1.5 km sandy beach with excellent wind funnelling from the Atlantic. Parking is straightforward; the beachfront has several car parks (€1–2 per hour). Launch from the northern end where the sand is widest, and watch for the occasional fishing nets in summer months. Water is clean, and the beach shelves gradually—perfect for learners.

Praia da Amorosa, just south, offers a more protected bay environment when northwesterlies are too strong. Ideal for flat-water tricks and light-wind sessions. Access via the coastal road; small free car park nearby.

Rio Minho Estuary (advanced riders only) provides light-wind opportunities and shelter from Atlantic swell. Launch from Âncora or Vila Nova on calm days. Tidal range is significant (3+ metres)—check tides before committing.

Hazards are minimal: occasional boat traffic in the estuary, summer fishing activity on Cabedelo's north end, and rocky outcrops at Praia da Amorosa's southern point. Stay clear of the port authority zone marked by buoys.

What Gear to Bring

Kite sizes: Pack 14m and 17m year-round. Winter (November–February) favours 17m; summer (June–August) demands 14m or smaller. The estuary works beautifully with 12m in light wind. We stock Duotone and Cabrinha kites at Surf Store—both proven in Atlantic conditions.

Boards: A twin-tip freestyle/freeride hybrid (36–39L for most riders) handles Viana's varied terrain. If you're planning the estuary, add a directional or light-wind speciality board for flat-water days.

Wetsuits: 5/3mm spring/autumn, 6/4mm or 7/5mm winter. Water warms to 20°C by August, so a 3/2mm summer suit is viable. ION wetsuits from Surf Store offer exceptional durability in salt water and rocky shore situations common at Viana.

Boots: Beach breaks are sandy, but rocky points demand neoprene booties. Invest in a good pair.

title: Kitesurfing Sagres — Cape St. Vincent Spot Guide 2026
meta_title: Kitesurfing Sagres Cape St Vincent Portugal 2026

excerpt: Sagres, Portugal offers consistent Atlantic swells and powerful winds perfect for kitesurfing at Europe's south-western edge.
body_html:
Why Sagres, Portugal Is Worth the Trip

Sagres sits at the south-western tip of continental Europe, where the Atlantic meets the Mediterranean. It's a pilgrimage site for serious water sports enthusiasts, and for good reason. The geography here creates a funnel effect—Atlantic storms and trade winds compress between Cape St Vincent and the African coast, producing some of Europe's most reliable and powerful wind conditions. You'll find consistent thermal winds even on days when the rest of southern Spain sits flat.

Beyond the wind, Sagres delivers raw, authentic Portugal. The landscape is dramatic: golden cliffs, hidden coves, and that sense of being at the edge of the world. Unlike crowded spots further north, Sagres attracts serious riders who respect the ocean's power. The water temperature stays pleasant year-round, and you'll share the lineup with real locals, not tourist masses.

If you're committed to kitesurfing Sagres, Cape St Vincent, Portugal, you'll experience one of the Atlantic's most dynamic playgrounds—and come away a better rider.

Best Spots & Access

Beliche Beach (Praia da Beliche) is the primary kitesurfing Sagres launch point. It's a sheltered cove on the western shore, protected from the heaviest Atlantic swell but still catching consistent wind funnelled around Cape St Vincent. Access is straightforward: head to the car park near the Beliche restaurant (GPS: 37.0045°N, 8.9440°W), walk down the stone steps for 5 minutes, and you're at the beach. The sand is firm, the take-off is clean, and there's a shallow bar perfect for tricks and progression.

Tonel Beach (Praia do Tonel), on the eastern side, faces the Mediterranean and gets lighter, more thermal winds. It suits beginners and light-wind sessions; access is via the coastal path from the old fortress. Beware: the current can push you east, so stay close to shore until you're confident.

Baleeira Harbour is a working fishing port with a small sandy beach. It's more sheltered but crowded with boats; only launch here in light conditions and watch for fishing lines.

Hazards to know: Atlantic swell can be heavy year-round. Check local forecasts and don't launch in storm conditions. The current runs east along the coast; always have an exit plan. Rocks line some coves, so scout your landing zone before launching.

Wind & Weather by Season

Month Avg Wind (knots) Direction Water Temp (°C) Rating

February 17–21 W–SW 13 Excellent

The sweet spot for kitesurfing Sagres is October through March, when Atlantic storms and trade winds deliver consistent 16–21 knot sessions. Winter storms can push conditions to 25+ knots; only advanced riders should venture out. Summer is lighter but more predictable for learning.

What Gear to Bring

Kite sizes: Pack a 14m for light days (May–June), a 12m for everyday conditions (the workhorse), and a 9m or 10m for winter storms. The funnel effect around Cape St Vincent means wind is usually powerful and consistent, so you'll rarely need oversized kites.

Board: Bring a freestyle or all-round board (135–145L for riders 70–90 kg). Sagres's shallow bar is perfect for tricks, but swell can be chunky, so a responsive board with decent rocker helps.

Wetsuit: Water temperatures range from 13°C (winter) to 21°C (summer). A 4/3mm winter suit is essential for November–March; a 3/2mm works for April–October. The Atlantic wind chill is real—don't skimp on thickness.

Extras: Helmet and impact vest are strongly recommended. Bring a rashguard or thermal base layer under your suit. A dry bag for car keys is a must; parking is secure but not supervised.

title: Kitesurfing Sardinia — Porto Pollo Spot Guide 2026

excerpt: Porto Pollo delivers world-class conditions for both kitesurfing and windsurfing with thermal winds, shallow bay launches, and year-round water sports potent...
body_html:
Why Sardinia — Porto Pollo, Italy Is Worth the Trip

Porto Pollo sits on Sardinia's northeast coast, and it's become one of Europe's most reliable destinations for both kitesurfing and windsurfing. The bay faces northeast, funnelling thermal winds that build predictably from late morning through early evening. You'll find protected, shallow water perfect for progression, yet enough fetch for intermediate and advanced riders to push their limits. The scenery—turquoise shallows, granite peaks, and pinewood forests—feels Mediterranean without the crowds you'd encounter at busier European spots.

What makes Porto Pollo special for kitesurfing, windsurfing, Sardinia

Best Spots & Access

Porto Pollo's main launch sits at the southern end of the bay, with dedicated sandy beach and shallow water extending 200 metres out. It's ideal for launching kites and boards without fighting shore breaks. Parking is straightforward—a small lot sits directly behind the beach, and the area is well signposted from the coastal road.

The bay itself is divided into zones. Beginners and schools typically use the southern shallows; intermediate riders favour the central bay where wind strengthens offshore; advanced sailors and kitesurfing Sardinia enthusiasts head to the northern point where the bay deepens and wind accelerates around headlands. Watch for the occasional small ferry traffic; locals will brief you on restricted areas.

Access is via SS427 from Palau (15 km away). Facilities include a handful of rental shops, a café, and basic amenities. The beach is public throughout, though busy from June to August. Hazards are minimal in summer, though autumn swell can create choppy conditions in the bay itself.

What Gear to Bring

For kitesurfing: Pack 14–17 m² kites for spring (thermal builds through the day); bring 12 m² for summer light winds and 17–19 m² for autumn storms. A twin-tip or directional board in 36–40 litres suits the bay's flat-water characteristics. Most riders favour freestyle or freestyle-wave hybrids here.

For windsurfing: Bring two sails—a 4.5 m² for lighter summer days and a 5.8 m² for spring and autumn punch. A 75–85 litre wave or freeride board handles chop and shallow water beautifully.

Wetsuits: A 3/2 mm suffices May–September; switch to 4/3 mm or 5/4 mm from October–April. Winter water hovers around 12°C, so don't skimp on insulation.

Porto Pollo has capable rental shops, but if you're flying from mainland Europe, hiring locally saves luggage hassle for a week's trip. title: Kitesurfing Sicily — Best Spots & Wind Guide 2026
meta_title: Kitesurfing Sicily Best Spots Italy 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Discover top kitesurfing spots in Sicily, Italy. Wind forecasts, gear advice & launch tips. Shop kitesurfing equipment at Surf Store.
excerpt: Sicily offers world-class kitesurfing conditions with warm water, consistent winds, and stunning Mediterranean coastlines—discover the best spots and gear.
body_html:
Why Sicily, Italy Is Worth the Trip

Kitesurfing in Sicily delivers what few European destinations can match: warm Mediterranean water, reliable seasonal winds, and an unspoiled coastline where ancient Greek temples overlook your launch. Unlike crowded Central European spots, Sicily rewards dedicated kiters with consistent conditions from spring through autumn, genuine hospitality, and the chance to explore a region steeped in history between sessions.

The island's south and east coasts face Africa, catching consistent trade winds. Water temperatures stay swimmable year-round—18°C in winter, 26°C in summer—so you'll pack lighter wetsuits than the Atlantic. And unlike busy spot-hopping destinations, Sicily's kitesurfing community remains intimate; you'll meet locals, discover secret bays, and enjoy uncrowded waters even in peak season.

Best Spots & Access

Mondello Beach (near Palermo)
Sicily's most accessible kitesurfing spot, just 30 minutes from Palermo Airport. A long, sandy beach with a dedicated water-sports zone and flat water for learners. Park at the seafront car park; launch from the beach access point near the sports centre. Winds blow onshore, so conditions suit beginners and intermediate kiters. Watch for swimmers on busy weekends; best session times are early morning or weekday afternoons.

Capo San Vito (Northwest)
A gem for intermediate and advanced riders. Exposed to Atlantic swells and consistent northwesterly winds, this spot delivers wave-riding conditions rare in the Mediterranean. The village has restaurants and basic facilities; park near the beach entrance. Expect shallow water close to shore, then deep channels—ideal for freestyle and wave sessions. Hazards include rocks on the western edge; scout conditions before launching.

Gela Beach (South Coast)
One of Sicily's most reliable kitesurfing spots, with steady thermal and trade winds. Sandy launch, flat to choppy water depending on swell, and long left-hand waves for advanced riders. Ample parking near the sports club; facilities are basic but adequate. This is genuine, uncrowded kiting—you'll rarely see more than a handful of kiters. Best for intermediate+ riders who want consistent wind and room to experiment.

Marettimo Island (Egadi Islands)
A day trip from Trapani for adventurous kiters. Accessible by ferry, this tiny island offers exceptional wind and isolation. Spotty infrastructure means bring supplies and book ferries in advance. Conditions are raw and exposed; only for confident riders with solid skills.

What Gear to Bring

Kite & Board Sizes
For reliable thermal and trade winds, pack a 14m and 17m kite (or 12m and 15m if you're heavyweight). Wave spots like Capo San Vito reward smaller kites and directional boards; flat-water spots like Mondello suit freestyle twins or hydrofoil boards. A 2.5–3.5m² board covers most conditions.

Wetsuits
Winter (December–February) demands a 5/4mm or 4/3mm steamer. Spring and autumn suit 3/2mm suits; summer calls for a 2mm shorty or rash vest. Warm water means comfort over insulation—quality stretch neoprene from ION or NeilPryde will serve you better than thick, restrictive suits.

Bar & Harness
A responsive bar from Duotone or Cabrinha handles variable winds; pack a waist harness for all-day comfort. Bring backup lines and a repair kit—access to spares in Sicily can be limited.

Rent or Buy? Advice from Surf Store

If you're new to kitesurfing in Sicily, rent locally in Palermo or Trapani for your first week. Schools and rental shops know the spots and seasonal quirks. At Surf Store, we stock premium kitesurfing kites, boards, and harnesses from trusted brands like Duotone, Cabrinha, and Nobile. title: Kitesurfing Taranto Puglia — Spot Guide 2026
meta_title: Kitesurfing Taranto Puglia Italy 2026 | Spot Guide
meta_description: Discover kitesurfing in Taranto, Puglia. Best wind season, launch spots, gear tips & local knowledge. Find gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Taranto offers consistent thermals and rare flat-water sessions for kiteboarders. Learn the best season, launch points, and gear essentials.
body_html:
Why Taranto — Puglia, Italy Is Worth the Trip

Taranto sits at the heel of Italy's boot, where the Ionian Sea meets the Mar Piccolo (Small Sea), creating a unique geography that rewards kitesurfing taranto puglia italy adventurers. The old city is framed by two natural harbours and shallow basins that turn glassy on lighter-wind days—rare gems in Mediterranean water sports. Afternoon thermals build reliably from May through September, and you'll find fewer crowds than on the Adriatic coast or Sardinia.

The spot's biggest advantage is its protected bay character. Unlike exposed Atlantic reefs, Taranto's geography buffers Atlantic swells while funnelling consistent 12–18 knot winds through the straits. Combine that with warm water, a thriving local diving and sailing culture, and authentic Puglian seafood within walking distance of launch points, and you have a destination that rewards patience and planning.

Best Spots & Access

Mar Piccolo (Small Sea) is the primary zone for kitesurfing taranto puglia italy—a shallow, sheltered basin ideal for light-wind days and beginners. Launch from the public beach near the Torre Maggiore fortification on the west side; parking is available nearby, though spaces fill quickly in summer weekends. Hazards include anchored fishing boats, submerged nets in deeper channels, and occasional swimmers. Always scout conditions from the waterfront café first.

Isola di San Pietro (Peter's Island) sits in the centre of the bay and creates a wind lane on its north shore. Access via boat or paddle from the Torre Maggiore beach; this spot suits intermediate riders seeking slightly more wind and deeper water. Keep clear of the island's marine reserve boundaries and watch for small boat traffic.

Rondinella Beach, south of the old city, offers stronger winds and faster water. It's rockier—wear board booties and check tide conditions before launching. Parking is limited; arrive early or use the harbour-side car park near the naval base (ask locals for the safest access route).

Wind & Weather by Season

MonthAvg Wind (knots)DirectionWater Temp (°C)Rating

May12NE–E19Good

June through August is peak season for kitesurfing taranto puglia italy, with thermal northeasterlies building daily. Winter sees stronger Atlantic lows from November–February, but conditions are less predictable. Spring and autumn offer pleasant mid-range winds and fewer tourists.

What Gear to Bring

Kite sizes: Pack a 14–17 m² for summer thermals (lighter, steady winds); a 12 m² for spring and autumn; and a 10–12 m² for winter Atlantic swells. Beginners should favour larger sizes for forgiveness in light conditions.

Boards: Shallow-water wave boards (90–95 litres) suit the bay; freeride twin-tips work in deeper zones. Bring a 70–80 litre freestyle board if tricks interest you.

Wetsuits: June–September requires only a 2 mm shorty or rash guard. October–May, wear a 3/2 mm full suit; January–March calls for a 4/3 mm or 5/4 mm. An extra hood helps on winter mornings.

Spares: Bring backup lines, repair tape, and a spare pump. The nearest professional repair shop is in Bari, 100 km north.

Rent or Buy? Advice from Surf Store

If you're visiting for a week, renting locally or from Bari saves money and hassle. However, Taranto's rental fleet is modest and quality inconsistent. We recommend purchasing or bringing your own kit from home—quality gear from brands like Duotone, Cabrinha, and ION performs reliably in Mediterranean conditions and pays for itself over repeated trips. title: Kitesurfing Leucate France — Spot & Wind Guide 2026

excerpt: Leucate offers consistent wind, shallow launch zones, and a relaxed Mediterranean vibe—ideal for progression and freestyle. Here's what you need to know.
body_html:
Why Leucate, France Is Worth the Trip

Leucate sits on the Languedoc coast where the Mediterranean meets reliable Tramontane and Mistral winds. Unlike crowded Atlantic resorts, kitesurfing Leucate France delivers a more authentic experience: shallow water, sandy flats perfect for tricks, and a tight-knit local crew. The lagoon itself is protected from ocean swell, making it ideal for wave-free progression sessions or freestyle competitions. Summer thermals bring consistent afternoon gusts, whilst winter Atlantic lows ensure year-round possibilities.

The village maintains a relaxed Mediterranean pace with excellent local restaurants, a small marina, and direct road access from Spain. You'll find genuine water sports culture here rather than a tourist circus.

Best Spots & Access

Leucate Plage (Main Beach) is the primary launch zone. Park near the seafront promenade; facilities include showers and a small shop. The beach shelves gradually, offering 50–150 metres of safe water before deeper channels. Morning sessions are flattest; afternoon Tramontane kicks hard by 15:00.

Salses-Leucate Lagoon is the real gem for kitesurfing Leucate France progression. Access via the southern causeway (small car park). Extremely shallow (0.5–2 metres), perfect for tricks, foiling, and learning. Watch for shallow-water launch technique and keep away from anchored sailboats. Weekday mornings are quieter.

Both spots face north-west; watch for power lines near the lagoon edge and respect the small fishing fleet. Swim-back potential is excellent on both days.

What Gear to Bring

Kite Sizes: Winter (Oct–Mar): 14–17 m² (heavier wind, stronger riders). Spring/autumn: 15–17 m². Summer: 12–15 m² (thermals lighter). Bring two quivers for Leucate's variable gusts.

Board: Twin-tip freestyle (35–41 L) for lagoon work; light wave board (40–50 L) if you venture to the beach for small Atlantic swell. Directional boards excel on foil days.

Wetsuits: Winter 5/4 mm hooded (9–10°C). Spring/autumn 3/2 mm. Summer: rash guard or 1 mm spring suit (18–21°C). Surf Store stocks ION thermal suits—locals recommend them for the Tramontane chill.

Rent or Buy? Advice from Surf Store

If you're visiting Leucate for a week, renting locally (two shops operate seasonally) works well for complete quivers. However, if you're serious about kitesurfing Leucate France progression, owning your own mid-size kite (15 m²) and freestyle twin-tip makes sense—rental gear is often dated, and Leucate's thermal afternoons demand quick rig decisions.

We recommend newcomers start with a trusted all-rounder like a Duotone Rebel or Cabrinha Switchblade (15 m²) paired with a Fanatic Wave or JP Australia board. title: Kitesurfing La Torche Brittany — Spot Guide 2026

excerpt: La Torche is a world-class kitesurfing destination in Brittany with consistent Atlantic swells, flat bays, and a vibrant water sports community.
body_html:
Why La Torche — Brittany, France Is Worth the Trip

La Torche sits on the Finistère peninsula in southwest Brittany, and it's become one of Europe's most reliable kitesurfing la torche brittany france destinations. You get consistent Atlantic wind, wave options for every skill level, and a genuinely welcoming local community. The bay is sheltered enough for learners, yet the nearby reefs and beach breaks attract advanced riders hunting for air time and tricks.

The landscape is quintessentially Breton—rocky headlands, golden sand, and seafood restaurants within walking distance. Unlike crowded Mediterranean spots, La Torche keeps its character. Water sports schools and local kite shops understand what you need, and the infrastructure (car parks, facilities, launching areas) is purpose-built. If you're serious about kitesurfing, this is a destination that rewards repeat visits.

Best Spots & Access

The main launching area is La Plage de La Torche itself—a wide, sandy beach with designated kite zones. Park near the Maison de la Torche visitor centre; parking is abundant and inexpensive. The beach has full facilities: showers, toilets, a small supermarket, and restaurants.

For learners and light-wind sessions, the Baie de Douarnenez (northern aspect) offers flatter water and deeper wind corridors. Access is straightforward via Douarnenez town (20 km north).

Advanced riders should explore Pointe de Penmarc'h, just south of La Torche. This exposed headland funnels Atlantic swells and creates powerful side-shore to side-onshore conditions. Respect the rocks and currents; only go here on confident wind days (18+ knots) and check local tide charts.

Hazards include rocks in the intertidal zone, tide rips near Penmarc'h, and occasional boat traffic. Always scout the beach in person before launching, and talk to the local spot monitors—they know the daily shifts better than any forecast.

Best season: November to March. Winter Atlantic storms deliver consistent 18–24 knot winds, moderate swells, and small crowds. Spring and summer are light-wind months; pack a large kite (17–19 m²) if you visit May–August.

What Gear to Bring

Kites: A 14 m² all-rounder (like a Duotone Vegas or Cabrinha Karma) covers most conditions. In winter, 12 m² is ideal for 20+ knot days. Light-wind enthusiasts should bring a 17 or 19 m² for summer visits.

Boards: A 135–145 L directional or freestyle board suits the bay's mixed conditions. If you're exploring Penmarc'h and want wave riding, add a 95–110 L wave-specific board (NeilPryde or Fanatic models excel here).

Wetsuits: January–March and October–November call for 5/3 mm full suits. April, September, and October: 4/3 mm. May–August: 3/2 mm shorties or spring suits. Water temperature climbs to 16–17 °C mid-summer but never gets truly warm.
