Spring (March–April) offers good conditions with rising water temperatures. Summer is lighter and more variable—expect glassy mornings and afternoon sea breezes rather than powered sessions. Winter winds are strong but water temperature drops below 5°C, demanding thick wetsuits.

What Gear to Bring

Kite sizes: March–April and September–November: bring 14–17 m² quads or freestyle kites. May–August: plan for 12–15 m² or have a wave/hydrofoil setup. January–February: 16–19 m² for light-wind days; 9–12 m² for heavy gusts.

Boards: Twin-tips and directional hybrids excel here. Shallow, sandy launch means forgiving landings—ideal for learning tricks. If you're into wave kitesurfing, smaller directional boards (4'6"–5'0") work beautifully on gusty autumn swells.

Wetsuits: 5/4 mm October–April; 4/3 mm May–September. For winter sessions (January–February), consider a 6/5 mm or even 7 mm booties. Water temperature is genuinely cold by Mediterranean standards.

Bring a spare bar, extra lines, and a multi-tool. Varberg's bay is sheltered, but once you venture beyond the headlands, conditions accelerate quickly.

Rent or Buy? Advice from Surf Store

If you're visiting Varberg for a week or less, renting locally makes sense—several Gothenburg-based schools and shops can deliver kit to your accommodation. Buying allows you to dial in board flex, kite response, and wetsuit thickness to Varberg's specific conditions.

Why Europe's Best Kitesurfing Destinations 2026 Is Worth the Trip

Europe's best kitesurfing destinations 2026 deliver consistent wind, shallow launch zones, and year-round access that rival any global hotspot. Unlike remote tropical breaks, European spots offer excellent infrastructure, safety services, and proximity to major cities—meaning you can fly in for a weekend or base yourself for months. Whether you're chasing Atlantic swells in Portugal, thermal winds in Greece, or the legendary thermals of the Mediterranean, Europe's kitesurfing scene has matured into a serious alternative to Caribbean and Red Sea destinations.

The continent's geography creates natural wind corridors. Atlantic storms drive reliable trade winds into western Spain and Portugal; the Mistral funnels through the Rhône Valley into southern France; Mediterranean thermals build predictability in Greece and Croatia. Combined with warm summer waters, shallow bays perfect for progression, and vibrant local communities, Europe's best kitesurfing destinations 2026 are where serious riders train, learn, and compete.

Best Spots & Access

Tarifa, Spain: The gateway to Europe's kitesurfing scene. Punta Paloma and Valdevaqueros deliver non-stop Levante and Poniente winds. Launch directly from sand beaches; parking is easy and costs €5–10 per day. Watch for shipping lanes and strong currents near the strait.

Cabarete, Dominican Republic alternative—Cape Town, South Africa equivalent: For European riders seeking Med consistency, Spotli Beach, Greece (near Athens) and Laganas Bay, Zakynthos offer shallow, protected launch zones. Park free or €2–3 at designated spots. Hazards: reef patches and occasional jellyfish blooms.

Vilamoura, Portugal: Sheltered lagoon and ocean-facing beach. Reliable Nortada wind May–September. Free parking; launchpads are sandy and beginner-friendly. Shallow water extends 200m—ideal for progression.

Sète, France: Mistral corridor excellence. Launch from Plage de la Corniche or Plage du Castellas. €3–5 daily parking. Hazard: strong offshore gusts and shipping traffic.

body_html:
Why Canary Islands Kitesurfing Guide 2026 Is Worth the Trip

The Canary Islands deliver reliable, consistent wind and warm Atlantic conditions that make kitesurfing canary islands best island choice for European riders. Unlike seasonal European breaks, you'll find rideable wind 200+ days per year, year-round water temperatures between 16–22°C, and a thriving community of local and travelling athletes. The archipelago offers something for everyone: flat-water lagoons for freestyle and wave riding in the same session, uncrowded breaks, and modern accommodation within minutes of the water.

What sets the Canary Islands apart is diversity. Lanzarote specialises in flat water and speed; Fuerteventura's northern beaches deliver powerful Atlantic swell with thermal winds; Gran Canaria offers sheltered lagoons and wave spots; Tenerife has extreme wave riding and big-air potential. Whether you're building tricks on a freestyle board or hunting clean waves, the Canary Islands kitesurfing scene has perfected the infrastructure and conditions to match your ambition.

Best Spots & Access

Lanzarote (Optimal for Freestyle & Flatwater)
Famara and Caletón Blanco dominate the flat-water scene with protected lagoons and consistent 12–18-knot winds. Launch at Famara beach; parking is plentiful and the lagoon is 200 metres offshore. Caletón Blanco (near Teguise) offers shallower water ideal for progression. Both sites have cafés, rental operators, and rescue cover.

Fuerteventura (Wave & All-Round)
Flag Beach and Sotavento are the flagships. Flag (north coast) catches Atlantic swell and north-east trade wind; expect 15–25 knots and challenging conditions. Sotavento Lagoon (south coast) offers calmer water on lighter days. Parking is easy; launch zones are clearly marked. Bring a 4×4 or secure a local shuttle to reach more remote spots like Costa Calma.

Gran Canaria (Lagoon + Waves)
Bahía Feliz on the south-east coast combines flat-water freestyle with small-to-medium waves. Pozo Izquierdo on the east coast is a high-wind wave venue (20–28 knots) best suited to intermediate+ riders. Launch infrastructure is solid; both spots have restaurants and changing facilities.

Tenerife (Expert Waves & Big Air)
El Medano is the epicentre: powerful Atlantic swells, 18–30-knot winds, and a world-class competition beach. Launch at the dedicated area near the town; facilities include gyms, shops, and a strong kitesurfing culture. Conditions are intense; only venture here with solid wave skills.

  • Board Type: Pack a freestyle board (139–143L) for flat water and a wave board (120–135L) if you plan any swell sessions. Foiling is growing; a 75–95L foilboard opens new possibilities in lighter wind.

Why Best Wing Foiling Spots Europe 2026 Is Worth the Trip

Wing foiling has transformed European water sports, and 2026 is the year to chase the perfect conditions across the continent. The best wing foiling spots Europe offers a unique blend of consistent wind, protected launch zones, and vibrant communities. Whether you're a progression rider hunting 12–15 knot thermals or a freestyle enthusiast after choppy water play, Europe's coastlines deliver.

Best Spots & Access

Tarifa, Spain is the gateway to best wing foiling spots Europe. The Strait winds funnel 15–25 knots from June to September. Launch from Punta Paloma beach (free parking, 10-minute walk). Watch for commercial ferry traffic in the strait itself; stay within 500 metres of shore.

Leucate, France excels in autumn and spring. The Mistral delivers 15–20 knots consistently. Park at Plage de la Franqui, launch directly from sand. Water temperature dips in winter, so plan accordingly. Local schools rent Duotone and NeilPryde wings.

Vassiliki, Greece offers thermal wind acceleration in summer. Launch from the organised beach (€5 daily fee). Shallow, protected water suits progression. Afternoon gusts reach 18 knots by July. Accommodation clusters within walking distance.

Jastarnia, Poland is a secret gem for spring wing foiling. Baltic winds blow 12–16 knots May through June. Free public launch, minimal crowds, premium conditions. Water temps rise to 16°C by summer.

body_html:
Why Novigrad — Istria, Croatia Is Worth the Trip

Novigrad is one of Istria's hidden gems for kitesurfing in novigrad croatia, offering a rare combination of reliable wind, protected waters, and authentic Mediterranean character. Perched on the northwestern coast, this medieval fishing town catches consistent Bora winds funnelling down from the Alps, creating predictable conditions that work for both progression and freestyle. Unlike busier Croatian spots, Novigrad remains refreshingly low-key—you'll share the water with locals who respect the conditions rather than crowds.

The shallow turquoise bays are forgiving for learning, yet the open Adriatic fetch provides enough power for wave riding and trick attempts. Water clarity is excellent, visibility often exceeds 10 metres, and the seabed is mostly sand and seagrass. The town itself offers excellent seafood, wine bars, and family-friendly accommodation, making it ideal for a week-long water sports trip.

Best Spots & Access

The primary launch point is Laguna Beach, a sheltered bay immediately north of town with direct road access and ample free parking. Entry is straightforward—shallow sand bottom for 50 metres, then gradual deepening. Conditions here are most consistent in Bora (northeasterly) winds; on lighter days, the bay becomes choppy rather than unrideable.

For stronger winds and wave potential, head to Funtana beach, 3 km south. This exposed shoreline catches more swell and sustained Bora gusts, suiting intermediate+ riders. Parking is limited but available; arrive early on peak-wind days. Watch for occasional fishing nets and respect local boat traffic.

A third option is Barbariga, 8 km north—a quieter bay favoured by windsurfers, offering similar shallow-launch conditions to Laguna. All three spots are reachable within 15 minutes by car from Novigrad town centre. We recommend checking local wind forecasts and tide tables before heading out; contact us at Surf Store if you need pre-trip advice on conditions.

title: Kitesurfing Kühlungsborn Germany — Baltic Sea Guide 2026
meta_title: Kitesurfing Kühlungsborn Mecklenburg Germany 2026 Guide
meta_description: Expert guide to kitesurfing in Kühlungsborn, Germany. Best spots, seasonal wind, gear advice & travel tips. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: Discover why Kühlungsborn is Germany's premier kitesurfing destination with consistent Baltic winds, shallow launch zones, and family-friendly beaches.
body_html:
Why Kühlungsborn, Germany Is Worth the Trip

Kühlungsborn sits on the Baltic coast of Mecklenburg-Vorpommern, just two hours north of Berlin, making it one of Germany's most accessible and consistent kitesurfing destinations. The shallow, protected bay offers ideal learning conditions for beginners whilst strong prevailing westerly and northwesterly winds provide exciting sessions for experienced riders. Unlike crowded Alpine lakes, Kühlungsborn's vast beach and calm approach zones mean you can rig up without stress and launch within minutes—no queuing, no traffic jams on the water.

The combination of sandy bottom, gentle slope, and excellent water clarity makes this Baltic hotspot perfect for progression. Many European riders visit Kühlungsborn specifically because the wind is reliable, the local community is welcoming, and the infrastructure—cafés, restaurants, and beachside facilities—rivals any premium European resort. Best of all, kitesurfing Kühlungsborn Mecklenburg Germany attracts fewer crowds than the North Sea spots, giving you more space to learn and progress.

Best Spots & Access

The main launch point is Kühlungsborn Ost, the eastern beach area near the promenade, where the sandy beach is wide and the water shallow for over 200 metres. This is your go-to for light winds and learning. Park at the beachfront car park (small fee) and you're rigged in under five minutes. The beach has lifeguards in summer and plenty of amenities.

Kühlungsborn West offers more space and slightly stronger, more organised wind—ideal for intermediate to advanced riders seeking flat-water freestyle or wave riding when swell wraps around from the northwest. Access is via the western promenade; parking is plentiful and free in shoulder seasons.

For serious wind, Redwitz Bay (15 km west) picks up significantly more pressure and is favoured by experts on strong days. The approach can be choppier but the water remains warm compared to northern alternatives.

Hazards are minimal: watch for swimmers in summer, keep clear of the marked shipping lane to the east, and respect the no-kite zones near the pier at peak tourism season. The sandy bottom means few rocks or debris—a major safety advantage. Avoid launch during severe thunderstorms; the flat terrain offers no shelter.

Wind & Weather by Season

February 13–17 W/NW 3–4 ★★★★☆

July 7–10 NE/W 17–19 ★☆☆☆☆
August 8–11 W/NE 17–19 ★★☆☆☆
September 10–13 W/NW 15–17 ★★★☆☆
October 12–16 W/NW 12–14 ★★★★☆
November 13–18 W/NW 9–11 ★★★★★
December 12–17 W/NW 5–7 ★★★★☆

Best season for kitesurfing Kühlungsborn Mecklenburg Germany is October to March. Autumn and winter bring consistent westerly gales; November is peak wind season. Summer is light and frustrating—plan a beach holiday instead. Water temperatures require a 5/3 mm winter suit (December–March) and 3/2 mm spring/autumn. July–August demand only 1.5 mm rashguards.

What Gear to Bring

For kite sizes: bring 14–17 m² for winter sessions (November–February); 12–14 m² for autumn and spring; 9–12 m² for lighter summer conditions. Dual-purpose 13 m² is the single best choice if you're visiting once yearly.

Board type: a 135–145 litre hybrid or all-round board handles Kühlungsborn's varied conditions beautifully. The shallow bay forgives mistakes and the water is forgiving for learning. Avoid ultra-light freestyle boards in winter; the chop demands stability.

Wetsuits: 5/3 mm with gloves and booties (December–February); 4/3 mm (March–April, September–October); 3/2 mm (May, August–September); 1.5 mm rashguard (June–July). The Baltic is cold even in summer.

Bring sunscreen, a dry bag, and a spare leash. The beach facilities are excellent but local rental shops can sell out during peak weekends, so owning or pre-booking gear is smart.

Rent or Buy? Advice from Surf Store

If you're visiting Kühlungsborn once, rent locally—many schools and beach shops hire solid kites (Duotone, Cabrinha, NeilPryde) and boards. Rental is €30–50 per day. However, if you're serious about progression or plan multiple trips, buying from Surf Store gives you familiar, tuned equipment that matches your style. We stock Duotone, NeilPryde, and Cabrinha kites, plus Fanatic and JP Australia boards—all chosen by our team based on real Baltic experience. Owning your gear also eliminates rental queues and rental damage stress.

For Kühlungsborn specifically, we recommend starting with our entry-level packages: a 13 m² Duotone Rebel, a 140 litre Fanatic Quad, and a quality harness. This trio costs less than a week's rentals and delivers better control, confidence, and progression. Shop now at Surf Store to kit yourself out before your trip—we ship across Europe in 2–3 days.

title: Kitesurfing Almería Cabo de Gata — Spot Guide 2026
meta_title: Kitesurfing Almería Spain Cabo de Gata 2026
meta_description: Discover kitesurfing at Cabo de Gata, Almería. Reliable wind, stunning desert landscape, and beginner-friendly conditions. Shop gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Cabo de Gata offers consistent wind, warm water, and dramatic coastal scenery—a world-class kitesurfing destination in southern Spain.
body_html:
Why Almería — Cabo de Gata, Spain Is Worth the Trip

Kitesurfing Almería Spain, specifically around Cabo de Gata, combines year-round wind, crystalline Mediterranean water, and a landscape that feels untouched. This natural park—Spain's only coastal desert—delivers reliable thermal winds from spring through autumn, consistent trade winds in winter, and water temperatures that rarely drop below 13°C. The shallow lagoons and wide sandy beaches make it ideal for progression, while the dramatic cliffs and secluded coves reward adventurous riders.

Unlike overcrowded spots further north, Cabo de Gata remains relatively quiet. Local infrastructure is simple but friendly; the area attracts serious enthusiasts and beginners alike. Most riders find the conditions forgiving enough to push progression without the stress of heavy crowds or dangerous tides.

Best Spots & Access

The primary launch for kitesurfing Almería is Playa del Pozo, a wide, sandy beach 15 km south of the town of Almería. It offers shallow water, consistent wind, and ample parking at the beach entrance. Start here if you're new to the spot; it's beginner-friendly and has a small café for post-session drinks.

Playa de los Muertos (north side) suits intermediate riders seeking slightly stronger wind and more swell. Launch from the small beach car park; watch for underwater rocks near the southern end.

San José beach (west, within the national park) offers protected conditions on lighter-wind days and is excellent for racing or freestyle. Parking is limited; arrive early.

Hazards: Underwater rocks are common near the cliffs; scan the takeoff zone before launching. The park is strictly protected; stay in designated water-sports areas and respect wildlife zones. Water entry fees apply at some beaches (€3–5 per day).

Wind & Weather by Season

Best season: October–March. Trade winds are strongest and most consistent; water is cool but refreshing. Summer (June–August) is light and thermal-driven—great for beginners, challenging for experienced riders.

What Gear to Bring

For kitesurfing Almería conditions:

  • Kite sizes: 14–17 m² in winter (December–February); 12–15 m² in autumn and spring; 9–13 m² in summer thermal winds. A directional or hybrid board suits the shallow waters and light thermals.
  • Board: Wave or freestyle boards work well. The sandy bottom and consistent swell favour freestyle progression.
  • Wetsuit: 3/2 mm or 4/3 mm October–April; 2/2 mm May–September. The water cools quickly in winter, so don't underestimate thickness.
  • Safety gear: Impact vest, helmet, and a leash are essential. Rocks and shallow water demand awareness.

Check conditions at local forecasting sites (Windguru, Magic Seaweed) before each session.

Rent or Buy? Advice from Surf Store

If you're visiting for a week, renting locally saves luggage hassle—several shops in Almería town rent decent equipment. However, if you're a regular or plan multiple trips, investing in your own gear from Surf Store makes sense. We stock top brands like Duotone, Cabrinha, and NeilPryde kites, JP Australia and Fanatic boards, and premium ION wetsuits. Our staff have real on-water experience in Mediterranean conditions and can advise on the exact setup for Cabo de Gata.

Order online and we'll ship across Europe in EUR. Save the guesswork—let us help you dial in your Almería quiver.

title: Kitesurfing Corsica France — Best Spots Guide 2026
meta_title: Kitesurfing & Windsurfing Corsica France 2026 Guide
meta_description: Discover Corsica's best kitesurfing and windsurfing spots, wind patterns, and gear tips. Expert guide for European water sports. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: Corsica offers consistent Mediterranean wind, turquoise lagoons, and world-class kitesurfing and windsurfing conditions. Here's your complete guide to spots,...
body_html:
Why Corsica, France Is Worth the Trip

Corsica is one of Europe's most rewarding destinations for kitesurfing and windsurfing. The island's unique geography—surrounded by deep blue waters and blessed with reliable thermals and coastal breezes—creates perfect conditions for both disciplines year-round. Unlike many European spots that depend on Atlantic swells, Corsica's wind patterns are consistent, predictable, and suitable for intermediate to advanced riders.

What sets Corsica apart is the combination of excellent wind, shallow lagoons ideal for progression, and the stunning backdrop of dramatic granite cliffs and pine-covered hills. You'll find fewer crowds than at traditional French Atlantic spots, warmer water in summer, and a welcoming local community. Whether you're refining your freestyle, cruising, or learning wave riding, kitesurfing and windsurfing in Corsica offers variety within a single trip.

Best Spots & Access

Biguglia Lagoon (near Bastia) is the premier flat-water spot for kitesurfing and windsurfing beginners and freestyle riders. The lagoon is massive, shallow (1–3 metres), and protected from swell. Launch from the northern shore near the village of Lucciana. Parking is available; bring a small cart for the 100-metre walk to the water.

Macinaggio (north coast) is ideal for intermediate kitesurfers seeking more space and stronger wind. The bay offers a mix of flat water and small chop. Access is straightforward via the D80 coastal road; free parking near the beach.

Bonifacio Strait (south coast) provides wave-riding opportunities when conditions align. The water is cooler and choppier than northern lagoons, but wind funnels through the strait reliably. Launch from Palau or Bonifacio town beaches. Respect local maritime traffic and strong currents in the strait itself.

Calvi Bay (northwest) offers a scenic, protected launch for kitesurfing and windsurfing with stunning views of the Citadelle. Summer thermals build throughout the day. Parking is limited in town; arrive early or use the beach car park 1 km south.

All spots are accessible by car; Bastia and Ajaccio airports serve the island. Bring your own equipment or consider renting locally to avoid excess baggage charges.

Wind & Weather by Season

Best season: October–November and June–August. Autumn brings stronger, gustier NW winds perfect for wave riding; summer offers thermal winds and warm water ideal for progression.

What Gear to Bring

Kites: A 13m and 17m will cover 90% of Corsican sessions. Autumn (October–November) demands 15–17m; summer thermals suit 12–14m. Bring a spare bladder.

Windsurfing sails: A 4.5m (summer) and 5.5m (spring/autumn) are versatile choices. Flat-water spots suit higher aspect-ratio sails; wave bays benefit from freeride cuts.

Boards: A 140L directional board covers light-wind days; a 100L wave board excels at Bonifacio. For progression, a 135L freeride twin-tip is ideal.

Wetsuits: Winter (November–March) requires a 5/3mm steamer. Summer (June–August) a 3/2mm or shorty suffices. Spring and autumn demand a 4/3mm.

Buy premium gear from Surf Store in Slovenia—we stock Duotone, Cabrinha, NeilPryde, ION, and Fanatic. Order before your trip and save on luggage fees.

Rent or Buy? Advice from Surf Store

If you're flying from the UK or mainland Europe, renting kitesurfing and windsurfing gear locally is practical. Corsica has rental shops in Bastia and Calvi, though stock varies seasonally. However, renters often compromise on quality and fit. Our recommendation: purchase your personal gear (harness, helmet, booties) before arrival, then rent a kite or sail locally. This hybrid approach ensures safety while saving weight in your luggage. Beginners benefit most from local instruction paired with familiar rental equipment.

For serious sessions, bring your own. Visit surf-store.com to buy trusted brands at European prices and ship to your Corsican accommodation ahead of your trip. We've helped hundreds of water sports enthusiasts prepare for island adventures—let our team help you select the right size and type for your level and the season you're visiting.

title: Kitesurfing Tarragona Costa Daurada — Spot Guide 2026
meta_title: Kitesurfing & SUP Tarragona Costa Daurada 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Discover kitesurfing and SUP at Tarragona's Costa Daurada. Wind patterns, launch spots, gear guide & local tips. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: Tarragona's Costa Daurada offers consistent Mediterranean wind, sandy launch spots, and sheltered bays perfect for kitesurfing, SUP, and wing foiling.
body_html:
Why Tarragona — Costa Daurada, Spain Is Worth the Trip

Tarragona's Costa Daurada coastline is a hidden gem for kitesurfing, SUP, and wing foiling across Southern Catalonia. Unlike the crowded breaks of the Atlantic coast, this Mediterranean destination delivers consistent thermal winds from late spring through early autumn, warm water, and a blend of sandy beaches and rocky coves that suit every skill level. The region offers something rare: reliable afternoon thermals without the winter cold of northern Europe, combined with excellent infrastructure, affordable accommodation, and a thriving local water sports community.

Whether you're chasing consistent sideshore wind, flat-water SUP sessions, or learning-friendly conditions, kitesurfing in Tarragona and the Costa Daurada delivers year-round opportunities. The shallow, sandy bays reduce hazard anxiety for beginners, while the thermal wind patterns reward patience and local knowledge.

Best Spots & Access

Playa del Miracle (Tarragona city centre) is the flagship kitesurfing and SUP spot—a broad sandy beach with consistent sideshore-onshore wind, lifeguard presence, and easy parking near the old town. Launch from the southern end; avoid the rocky breakwater to the north. Water depth suits all levels, and the beach has shower facilities and nearby restaurants.

Playa Llarga (15 km south, Salou direction) is the best flat-water SUP and wing foiling destination. The sheltered bay funnels thermals beautifully and offers a longer fetch for foiling. Park at the beach car park; facilities are basic but reliable.

Cala Fonda (30 km south, near Cambrils) is a rockier, more sheltered cove favoured by wave-hunting kiters when Atlantic swells wrap around. Parking is tight; arrive early or use the nearby Cambrils marina car park (500 m walk). Underwater rocks mean this spot is best for intermediate+ riders.

All spots are accessible year-round; summer crowds are heaviest June–August. Winter sees occasional Atlantic swells but reduced thermal wind. Check local tide tables; tidal range is small (less than 1 m), but sandbanks shift seasonally.

Wind & Weather by Season

November 9–13 NW, variable 16 ★★☆
December 8–11 NW, calm 14 ★★☆

Peak season: May–June. Thermal winds arrive reliably by mid-morning, build to 15–20 knots by afternoon, and drop after sunset. July–August offer the warmest water but lighter, more variable winds. Spring (April–May) and autumn (September–October) deliver the strongest, most consistent wind for kitesurfing on the Costa Daurada.

What Gear to Bring

Kite sizes: Thermal-wind specialists bring 14–17 m kites for light mornings; 12–14 m for mid-peak season. Backup a 9–12 m for lulls or strong Atlantic days. SUP and wing foiling benefit from 5–7 m wings in thermal conditions.

Boards: Directional wave kites suit Cala Fonda's Atlantic swells; flat-water twin-tips dominate Playa Llarga and Miracle. Twin-tips (36–41 L) are safest for learners; experienced riders choose directionals (38–45 L) for more float in light wind.

Wetsuits: May–September, a 3/2 mm springsuit covers most sessions. April and October require 4/3 mm. November–March, pack a 5/4 mm steamer or seal if you plan extended trips. The thermal winds mean you'll be out longer than you expect.

Bring a helmet (mandatory on many Spanish beaches), check local club rules, and carry a rashguard—the sun reflects hard off the Mediterranean.

Rent or Buy? Advice from Surf Store

If you're visiting once, rent locally or shop Tarragona's specialist shops. If you're based in Europe and travel regularly to the Costa Daurada, buying from Surf Store saves money, guarantees equipment familiarity, and supports expert stock curation. Our team has logged hundreds of hours on these beaches; we'll advise exact sizes before you book a trip. Shop now at Surf Store and arrive with kit dialled to Tarragona conditions.
